text: noting how another French intellectual later in the twentieth century similarly left Paris and France in order to take up peripheral teaching and administrative positions for the French state in the far North, who lived for a shorter period in Poland (and later North Africa) and who also managed to do decisive research next to holding full-time administrative jobs – and whose surgeon father had mounted pressure on his son to become a doctor. Both would eventually drop the part of their name that made reference to their father. Both would return to the heart of French academia and eventually apply for a position at the Collège de France. Charles-Arnold became Arnold and Paul-Michel became Michel. The difference is that Foucault got the job. 9 As discussed by Belmont (1979: 7–9), in this same article van Gennep presents an indeed remarkable homemade theory of language learning. The first rule is to focus on the sounds and rhythms of a language, training the ear, and then exercising one’s muscles in Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 35 In 1903 his wife was pregnant again, and afraid to lose the child for a second time, the couple decided to move out of the city, to quieter Clamart just outside Paris. It meant a lot of commuting by train for van Gennep. A few years later the family would try to return to Paris, but as their young daughter, Ketty, got seriously ill with pleurisy their doctor advised them to return to the province. They did, but this time to Bourg-la-Reine, where train connections to the Ministry of Agriculture were better. Van Gennep would live most of his remaining life in Bourg-la-Reine; and here both he and his wife would die. There is a small street named after him just two blocks off the train station from where he commuted, Rue Arnold van Gennep. It is the only street, square or monument in the world stamped with his name. He did leave his mark somewhere, after all, even if one has to search quite hard for it. Paris and the Encounter with the French Social Sciences Van Gennep’s formal career now took place outside academia. Next to his ministerial position, he kept on translating and editing. For over thirty years, from 1906 to 1939, he edited, for a minor salary, the section Ethnographie-Folklore-Religions-Prehistoire in Mercure de France. However, upon his return to France, he still managed to advance his academic work. He was in fact amazingly productive considering the fact that he kept performing full- time as head of a ministry office. He also returned to his studies. Van Gennep now enrolled to study sciences religieuses with Léon Marillier at the École Pratique. He was in fine company once again, finer than ever, becoming part of that handful of young people who would later become Durkheim’s and Mauss’ collaborators: Mauss himself, Henri Hubert, Paul Fauconnet and many others. There is no doubt that Mauss and van Gennep were equally influenced by Marillier, and both took much inspiration from his insistence on a factually based social science, paying close attention to both detail and context, and never allowing ideas or theories to lift them away from the empirical ground. Upon Marillier’s sudden death in 1901, Marcel Mauss became van Gennep’s teacher and mentor, and therefore closely followed van Gennep in the work towards his first book. In 1903, Marcel Mauss proofread and thoroughly commented upon van Gennep’s thesis on taboo and totemism in Madagascar. Van Gennep’s interests during the first decade of the century would develop alongside those trying to imitate sounds and intonations, before moving on to the grammar, here starting out with the absolutely invariable elements and then progressing to the variable ones. Words, then, must be learned, not from their meaning, but from their root; in this way, any word summoned brings with it the whole linguistic cluster to which it belongs, whereas any direct reference to the meaning in the appropriation of a word will close the learner off from the larger semantic matrix from which that meaning emerges. One discerns, behind these rules, much of van Gennep’s ethnographic method. In fact, for him it was probably more or less the same thing. Liminality and the Modern 36 of the Durkheimians. This included the classical topics of totemism, taboo, the origins and nature of religion, magic, classification systems and the relationship between myth and ritual. In 1904 he succeeded where Mauss would constantly fail: he published his thesis in 1904 as Tabou et Totémisme à Madagascar. Étude Descriptive et Théorique. The book is dedicated to the memory of Léon Marillier. In the preface van Gennep reserves the final thanks to ‘mon ami Marcel Mauss’ (van Gennep 1904: 2). In this his first book, van Gennep approached two key questions of contemporary anthropology: taboos and totemic belief systems. Van Gennep here analysed totemism as a social system of classification, taking its role far beyond the more narrow field of religion. His understanding of Frazer’s merits and limits were already clearly formulated here. In 1906 he published his second book, Mythes et légendes d’Australie. It is one of the first books of the century openly to denounce analytical ethnocentrism and it was here also, as will be discussed in Chapter 2, that van Gennep began openly to expose the most blatant flaws in Durkheim’s work. Van Gennep had in the same period started to publish a flow of journal articles, all the while translating major works from English into French, most of which are still in use today. He published in French, Flemish, German and English, and in a wide plethora of journals. While van Gennep would continue to publish on animal branding, linguistics and more narrowly defined folkloric topics (he was a frequent contributor to Revues des Traditions Populaires), he would also start to publish material of a broader comparative relevance, as was the case for many of his articles published in Revues des Idées.
